one_line_takeaway: Safe RL algorithms that satisfy training-time cost constraints on a single training patient show large clinical safety degradation (TIR drops >10 points for most algorithms) when zero-shot evaluated on unseen diabetic patients in a new unified T1D/T2D simulator, and a proposed test-time predictive shield using a novel Basis-Adaptive Neural ODE dynamics model recovers much of that gap (up to +14% TIR) more reliably than a static rule-based shield.
key_finding: Safety generalization gap (Table 1, unshielded baselines): across all 8 algorithms, moving from the training patient (in-distribution) to unseen patients (OOD) drops TIR and worsens Risk Index for 7 of 8 algorithms -- e.g., CPO falls from 87.28% to 76.73% TIR (Delta TIR -10.55) with Risk Index rising from 4.09 to 5.72; FOCOPS falls from 88.08% to 75.42% TIR (Delta TIR -12.66). PCPO is the outlier, already performing poorly in-distribution (36.74% TIR) and showing a small positive Delta TIR (+2.01) OOD. Dynamics prediction (Table 2, 24-step/120-min horizon): BA-NODE achieves the lowest error of the three models compared -- MAE 2.82 +/- 0.01, FDE 3.63 +/- 0.03, RMSE 4.42 +/- 0.02, versus NODE (MAE 3.18, FDE 4.11, RMSE 5.10) and ITransformer (MAE 4.11, FDE 5.39, RMSE 7.25). Shielding effect by diabetes type: for T1D (Table 3), predictive shielding improves all baselines' TIR (average Delta TIR +6.08%) and reduces CV for every agent (e.g., FOCOPS Delta CV -4.26%), with CUP and PCPO as the only cases of slight TIR/Risk Index degradation (though CV still improves). For T2D-with-pump (Table 4), gains are largest: PPO-Lag TIR +14.15 (to 92.95%, Delta CV -5.50), CPO TIR +13.54 (to 95.05%, Delta CV -6.68), RCPO TIR +12.73. For T2D-without-pump (Table 5, referenced but not fully shown in extracted pages), gains are described as less consistent -- e.g., PPO-Lag improves TIR by +4.46% but CV worsens by +4.39%, while CRPO improves TIR by +11.80% and reduces CV by -7.28%. Predictive shielding is reported to outperform the static Rule-Based Shield in most settings, since RBS's rigid threshold corrections can overshoot and induce oscillatory hypo/hyperglycemia feedback loops; the exception is when the underlying base policy is already poor (CUP, PCPO), where the predictive shield -- being constrained by the base policy's action distribution -- is outperformed by the heuristic RBS.
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions (as stated by the authors, not related work) are threefold: (1) a Unified Diabetes Simulator covering Type 1 diabetes with pump therapy, Type 2 diabetes with pump therapy, and Type 2 diabetes without pump therapy, built on the UVA-Padova glucose-insulin model family and modeling a recommender agent that proposes discrete interventions (bolus insulin, meal intake) rather than direct continuous control; (2) an OOD Safety Benchmark that trains 8 safe RL algorithms on a single representative patient per cohort (Child#01, Adolescent#01, Adult#01) and evaluates zero-shot on 9 held-out patients (#02-#10) per diabetes type, revealing a 'safety generalization gap' where training-time constraint satisfaction (Risk Index < 5, TIR > 80%) degrades under distribution shift (TIR drops by more than 10% on unseen patients for most algorithms); and (3) Test-Time Predictive Shielding, an algorithm-agnostic runtime action-masking wrapper built on a new dynamics model called Basis-Adaptive Neural ODE (BA-NODE), which combines an ITransformer history encoder, an ensemble of K neural-ODE vector fields integrated via RK4, and Function-Encoder-style least-squares patient-specific basis-mixing weights to forecast glucose trajectories and prune actions predicted to cause hypo/hyperglycemia. The authors also give a probabilistic safety theorem (one-sided (epsilon, alpha)-reliability) showing that if the BA-NODE predictor's overestimation error is bounded, the shield guarantees a lower bound on the probability of avoiding hypoglycemia.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a safe reinforcement-learning / clinical-control paper, not an adversarial or AI-security paper. There is no adversary, attacker capability, or attack surface analyzed anywhere in the text -- the 'safety' discussed is control-theoretic/clinical safety (avoiding hypo/hyperglycemia under natural physiological distribution shift between a training patient and unseen patients), not security against a malicious actor. No threat model should be attributed to this paper.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (verbatim source, paraphrased): the framework targets physiological distribution shifts that are partially inferable from recent context; extreme, unannounced events (e.g., unannounced meals, acute stress) may invalidate the shield's dynamics forecasts. All evaluation is in a simulated clinical environment (UVA-Padova-derived simulator) -- no real or hybrid real-world clinical data was used, and the authors explicitly state that extending to real-world deployment requires validation under real/hybrid clinical data streams and prospective clinical protocols. The predictive shield's effectiveness is bounded by the base policy's own action distribution: when the underlying safe-RL controller performs poorly (as with CUP and PCPO, whose baseline TIR was well below the 80% target), the shield cannot fully compensate and a simple rule-based shield can outperform it. The authors also note future work should add explicit epistemic uncertainty modeling, tighter integration between shielding and policy learning, and study of sim-to-real transfer with a clinician in the loop -- implying these are not addressed in the current paper.

datasets_used: Simulator-generated trajectories from the authors' own Unified Diabetes Simulator (UVA-Padova mechanistic glucose-insulin model family, hybrid Hovorka+Dalla Man for T2D) -- no external/public patient dataset is used
9 patients per diabetes type/age cohort used for zero-shot OOD evaluation (Patients #02-#10), with a single patient per cohort (#01) used for training
15 days of simulated glucose trajectories per cohort (adult, adolescent, pediatric) for training the BA-NODE dynamics predictor, plus 5 additional simulated days held out for evaluation
quantitative_results: Table 1: unshielded generalization gap, e.g. CPO TIR 87.28% (ID) -> 76.73% (OOD), Delta TIR -10.55, Risk Index 4.09 -> 5.72; FOCOPS Delta TIR -12.66; PCPO the sole outlier with Delta TIR +2.01 (already low ID performance at 36.74% TIR)

Table 2 (24-step/120-min horizon dynamics prediction): BA-NODE MAE 2.82 +/- 0.01, FDE 3.63 +/- 0.03, RMSE 4.42 +/- 0.02, versus NODE MAE 3.18 / FDE 4.11 / RMSE 5.10 and ITransformer MAE 4.11 / FDE 5.39 / RMSE 7.25
Table 3 (T1D, shielded vs unshielded): average Delta TIR +6.08% across 8 algorithms; FOCOPS Delta CV -4.26%; CUP and PCPO show slight TIR/Risk Index degradation
Table 4 (T2D-with-pump, shielded vs unshielded): PPO-Lag Delta TIR +14.15 (TIR 92.95%, Delta CV -5.50); CPO Delta TIR +13.54 (TIR 95.05%, Delta CV -6.68); RCPO Delta TIR +12.73
T2D-without-pump (Table 5, text description): PPO-Lag Delta TIR +4.46% but Delta CV +4.39% (worse); CRPO Delta TIR +11.80%, Delta CV -7.28%
72 total experimental settings (8 safe RL algorithms x 3 diabetes types x 3 age groups)
baselines_compared: 8 safe RL algorithms: PPO-Lag, TRPO-Lag, CPO, RCPO, FOCOPS, PCPO, CRPO, CUP
Rule-Based Shield (RBS), mimicking Low Glucose Suspend (LGS) systems with fixed glucose/IOB thresholds
Dynamics-model baselines for BA-NODE: plain NODE and ITransformer
